criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the Median Truncated Copy Masking (MT-CM) methodology, including the definition of masked values, unmasked values, true missing values sets, and the rationale for extending naive copy masking to transformer-based MAEs.

The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand context awareness, including the partitioned embedding design (value component U and context component C), language model embedding generation, linear projections, and positional encoding.

The agent has read Section 3.3 and Appendix B to understand the full CACTI transformer backbone architecture, including the encoder selector function, decoder mask-reorder mechanism, and training optimization details.

The agent has read Algorithm 1 (Naive Copy Masking) completely to understand the row-wise permutation of the missingness mask matrix and the conditional application based on masking ratio p_cm and minimum one remaining observed feature.

The agent has read Algorithm 2 (MT-CM) completely to understand the median truncation mechanism: computing per-sample observed feature counts, computing batch-level median, truncating each sample to at most o_B^median observed features with random feature permutation, and padding with null tokens.
The agent has read Algorithms 3 and 4 (CACTI Training and Inference) to understand the full forward pass, loss computation, and inference pipeline.

The agent has read Section 4 to understand the evaluation setup: 10 UCI datasets, 80-20 train-test splits, three missingness mechanisms (MCAR, MAR, MNAR), primary evaluation at 30% missingness, and three evaluation metrics (R², RMSE, WD).
The agent has read Table 1 to understand the full benchmark results comparing CACTI and CMAE against 13 baseline methods under MCAR, MAR, and MNAR at 30% missingness.
The agent has read Table 2 (ablation analysis) to understand the four ablation model configurations: RMAE, RMAE+CTX, CMAE, and CACTI, and the results at 90% masking ratio over four datasets.

The agent has read Appendix C.2 to understand the default CACTI hyperparameter configuration: AdamW optimizer with lr=1e-3, betas=(0.90, 0.95), cosine annealing with 50 warmup epochs, gradient clipping at 5.0, 300 training epochs, batch size 128, p_cm=0.90, encoder depth Ne=10, decoder depth Nd=4, embedding width E=64, 8 attention heads, GTE-en-MLM-large context model with context ratio C/E=0.25.

The plan includes implementation of the naive copy masking strategy: generating a row-wise permutation of the observed missingness mask matrix M, and applying the permuted mask M^perm to each sample with probability p_cm if at least one feature remains observed.

The plan includes implementation of the MT-CM strategy: computing observed feature counts per sample after naive copy masking, computing the batch-level median o_B^median, truncating each sample's observed features to min(o_n, o_B^median), applying random per-sample feature permutation, and padding with null tokens when needed.

The plan includes generating context embeddings by passing column names and descriptions through a language model (GTE-en-MLM-large by default), averaging the last-layer hidden states across all tokens, and applying a linear projection to reduce to C-dimensional context embeddings.

The plan includes building the context-aware input embedding: splitting the total embedding dimension E into value portion U = 0.75E and context portion C = 0.25E, applying a linear projection for value embeddings, and concatenating with fixed sin-cosine positional embeddings: E_n = [U_n || C] + P.

The plan includes implementing the encoder-decoder transformer backbone with: encoder of Ne=10 self-attention blocks processing only the non-masked features after MT-CM selection, and decoder of Nd=4 self-attention blocks receiving the latent representation with [MASK] tokens for missing/masked features.

The plan includes the MSE reconstruction loss computed separately over observed features (L_O_n) and masked features (L_M_n), with the total loss being their sum, and min-max scaling of all inputs before training.

The plan includes the main benchmarking experiment setup: 10 UCI datasets, 80-20 train-test splits, missingness simulation under MCAR, MAR, and MNAR at 30% using the HyperImpute package, and evaluation using R², RMSE, and Wasserstein distance metrics.

The plan includes all 13 baseline methods: ReMasker, DiffPuter, AutoComplete, HyperImpute, MissForest, ICE, MICE, Sinkhorn, GAIN, MIWAE, notMIWAE, SoftImpute, and Mean, with their default hyperparameter configurations as listed in Table A7.

The plan includes the ablation study with four model configurations: RMAE (random masking, no context), RMAE+CTX (random masking, with context), CMAE (MT-CM, no context), and CACTI (MT-CM, with context), over four datasets (bike, default, spam, students) under MAR and MNAR at 90% masking ratio.

Naive copy masking is implemented correctly as in Algorithm 1: a random row-wise permutation π is applied to M to create M^perm, then for each sample i, with probability p_cm the copy mask M^cm_i is set to M^perm_i, but only if the bitwise product M^cm_i · M^perm_i ≥ 1 (ensuring at least one feature remains observed).

MT-CM is implemented correctly as in Algorithm 2: for each sample n in batch, o_n is computed as the sum of M^cm_n; the batch-level median o_B^median is computed; o_n^trunc = min(o_n, o_B^median); a random feature permutation π_n is applied; the first o_n^trunc observed features are retained as the observed set O_n and the rest of the observed (not truncated) go to masked set M_n; null token padding is added when o_n^trunc < o_B^median.

Context embeddings are generated by processing column names and descriptions through a pre-trained language model (GTE-en-MLM-large by default), averaging last-layer hidden states across all tokens (C_k^ci = (1/|T_k|) Σ Embd(t_ki)), and applying a linear projection r_e: C^ci → C to reduce to C-dimensional embeddings.

The context-aware input embedding is correctly constructed: value embeddings U_nk are produced by a linear projection l applied to each scalar feature value; U = 0.75E and C = 0.25E; the final embedding is E_n = [U_n || C] + P, where P are fixed sin-cosine positional embeddings.

The encoder is implemented as Ne=10 residual self-attention blocks that process only the non-missing/non-masked context-aware embeddings E_n' (after MT-CM selection), producing a latent representation L_n.

The decoder is implemented correctly: a separate linear projection r_d maps context embeddings C^ci to decoder context embeddings C'; the latent representation L_n is reordered and missing/masked features are filled with a fixed [MASK] vector projected by a linear layer to produce value embeddings V_n ∈ R^(U×K); the decoder input is Z_n = [V_n || C'] + P, processed by Nd=4 residual self-attention blocks followed by a 2-layer MLP to produce the imputed output X̄_n.

The training loss is implemented as MSE over observed features L_O_n = (1/|O_n|) Σ (x̃_nk - x̄_nk)² for k in O_n, plus MSE over masked features L_M_n = (1/|M_n|) Σ (x̃_nk - x̄_nk)² for k in M_n, with the total loss being L = L_O_n + L_M_n.
Input data is min-max scaled before passing to the model, and during inference the model output is inverse-transformed back to the original feature scale for continuous features.

The optimizer is AdamW with initial learning rate 1e-3, beta parameters (0.90, 0.95), gradient clipping threshold 5.0, cosine annealing LR scheduler with 50 warmup epochs, batch size 128, and 300 training epochs as default.

The CACTI training algorithm (Algorithm 3) is fully implemented: naive copy masking applied once per epoch across all samples, context-aware embeddings computed per batch, MT-CM applied per batch, encoder and decoder forward passes, MSE loss computed and averaged over batch, and weights updated via backpropagation.

The CACTI inference algorithm (Algorithm 4) is correctly implemented: all true missing features are dropped from the encoder input (no copy masking applied at inference), the decoder reconstructs all features, and the final imputed output replaces only the originally missing values (X̂_n = X̃_n ⊙ M_n + X̄_n ⊙ (1 - M_n)).

Missingness simulation is implemented for all three mechanisms using the HyperImpute package: MCAR (each value masked i.i.d. with fixed probability), MAR (subset of features fixed as fully observed; remaining features masked based on a logistic model), and MNAR (MAR inputs further masked with a Bernoulli variable).
All 10 UCI datasets are loaded and preprocessed with an 80-20 train-test split, supporting missingness simulation under MCAR, MAR, and MNAR at 10%, 30%, 50%, and 70% missingness proportions.
All three evaluation metrics are correctly computed on the original (un-scaled) feature values: Pearson's R² between imputed and true values, RMSE, and Wasserstein distance (WD).

All 13 baseline methods are implemented and runnable with their default (or recommended) hyperparameter settings as specified in Table A7: ReMasker, DiffPuter, AutoComplete, HyperImpute, MissForest, ICE, MICE, Sinkhorn, GAIN, MIWAE, notMIWAE, SoftImpute, and Mean.

The CMAE model (CACTI without context awareness) is implemented as a separate baseline that uses MT-CM but replaces context embeddings with no context, for use in ablation and comparison with ReMasker.

The RMAE model (random masking, no context) and RMAE+CTX model (random masking, with context) are implemented for ablation analysis, sharing the same transformer backbone as CACTI but using random masking instead of MT-CM.

The architecture sensitivity experiment is implemented: encoder depth Ne ∈ {4, 6, 8, 10, 12}, decoder depth Nd ∈ {4, 6, 8, 10, 12}, and embedding size E ∈ {32, 64, 128, 256, 512} are each varied while holding other hyperparameters constant, evaluated on four datasets (bike, default, spam, students) under MAR and MNAR at 30% missingness and 90% masking ratio.

The MT-CM masking rate sensitivity experiment is implemented: p_cm is varied across values including at least {0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 0.90, 0.95} for CMAE on four datasets under MAR and MNAR, and compared against naive copy masking at corresponding rates.

The context embedding model sensitivity experiment is implemented: at minimum BERT-base, BERT-large, DeBERTa-v3-base, DeBERTa-v3-large, GTE-en-MLM-large, and NV-Embed-v2 are evaluated as context embedding models on four datasets under MAR and MNAR at 30% missingness.

The main benchmarking experiment has been executed successfully for CACTI and all 13 baseline methods on all 10 datasets under MCAR, MAR, and MNAR at 30% missingness, producing R², RMSE, and WD metrics on both train and test splits.
The extended benchmarking experiments have been executed successfully at 10%, 50%, and 70% missingness proportions in addition to the primary 30% experiments, for all three missingness mechanisms.
The ablation analysis experiments have been executed successfully for RMAE, RMAE+CTX, CMAE, and CACTI on four datasets (bike, default, spam, students) under MAR and MNAR at 90% masking ratio.

The MT-CM vs. naive copy masking comparison experiments have been executed successfully at multiple masking rates on bike and obesity datasets under all three missingness scenarios at 30% missingness.
The architecture sensitivity analysis experiments have been executed without errors for all encoder depth, decoder depth, and embedding size configurations on the four ablation datasets.
The context embedding model sensitivity experiments have been executed successfully for all six embedding models on the four ablation datasets.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: CACTI outperforms all 13 baseline methods across all metrics (R², RMSE, WD) and all three missingness conditions (MCAR, MAR, MNAR) at 30% missingness, with CACTI R² of approximately 0.46/0.47/0.46 under MCAR/MAR/MNAR on the test split.
The execution outcomes match Table 1: the average relative R² improvement of CACTI over the next best method is approximately 5.3%, 6.1%, and 13.4% under MCAR, MAR, and MNAR respectively.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: CMAE consistently outperforms ReMasker across R² and RMSE metrics under all three missingness conditions, confirming that MT-CM alone provides a meaningful improvement over random masking.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: MT-CM provides approximately a 115% gain in MNAR R² over random masking (CMAE R²≈0.43 vs. RMAE R²≈0.20 under MNAR), and context awareness provides approximately a 30% MNAR R² gain over random masking when added to RMAE (RMAE+CTX R²≈0.26 vs. RMAE R²≈0.20).

The execution outcomes match Table 2: CACTI (MT-CM + context) achieves approximately a 5% further R² improvement over CMAE (MT-CM only), consistent with the paper reporting a nearly 5% improvement under MAR (0.46 vs. 0.46) and MNAR (0.45 vs. 0.44→0.43).

The execution outcomes match Table A6: for naive copy masking, increasing p_cm from 10% to 50% monotonically decreases R² performance; for MT-CM, increasing p_cm from 10% to 90% monotonically increases R² performance, with best MT-CM performance at p_cm=0.90 outperforming best naive copy masking by 6.7%, 5.8%, and 2.1% under MNAR, MAR, and MCAR respectively.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: CACTI is relatively insensitive to encoder depth and decoder depth choices, while embedding size E=64 yields the highest accuracy, and performance degrades significantly at E=512 due to overfitting.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: all six context embedding models yield comparable performance with marginal differences, GTE-en-MLM-large and BERT-large tend to achieve the highest accuracy, and DeBERTa-v3-large achieves the lowest accuracy, with no clear relationship between embedding model size and performance.
